text:
Transition metal oxides undergo dynamic surface reconstruction at the electrified interface to form the active state for the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). While electrolyte pH significantly influences catalytic performance, the underlying mechanisms governing this pH dependence remain poorly understood. In this study, we utilize operando X-ray absorption spectroscopy and in situ electrochemical analysis to monitor Co oxidation state changes at the (hydr)oxide/electrolyte interface across different pH regimes. 

Our findings demonstrate that interfacial Co redox dynamics are intrinsically linked to the flat band potential and surface reconstruction. In alkaline environments, a low flat band potential facilitates the Co redox transition at lower potentials, promoting efficient surface reconstruction. Conversely, moving to neutral or acidic conditions induces an anodic shift in Co redox transformation, which increases the catalytic overpotential. In particular, neutral electrolytes exhibit the highest overpotentials due to limited Co atom polarizability and sluggish oxidation state kinetics. By establishing a direct correlation between interfacial charge accumulation, Co oxidation dynamics, and the thermodynamic shifts of redox couples, this work clarifies how the pH-directed interface dictates OER activity. These insights offer a new framework for designing high-performance electrocatalysts by tuning interfacial polarizability and redox behavior tailored to specific pH environments.
